title: Rajesh Kumar Sharma v. InfoTech Solutions Pvt. Ltd.
keyArguments: The termination of the petitioner was carried out following due process as per the Standing Orders of the Company and the service agreement signed at the time of appointment.
The petitioner was issued show cause notices on three separate occasions and was given adequate opportunity to present his case before the disciplinary committee.
The petitioner's act of sharing confidential client data with a competitor firm constituted a fundamental breach of the employment contract and company policy.
The Labour Court's finding of fact regarding misconduct was based on cogent evidence including electronic communications and witness testimonies and does not warrant interference.
The petitioner failed to exhaust the internal grievance redressal mechanism before approaching the court, rendering the petition premature.
judgmentSummary: The High Court of Delhi dismissed the writ petition filed by Rajesh Kumar Sharma challenging his termination from InfoTech Solutions Pvt. Ltd. The court upheld the findings of the Labour Court and affirmed that the dismissal was lawful, following due process, and based on proven misconduct involving unauthorized disclosure of confidential client data. The court found no jurisdictional error or perversity in the Labour Court's award and declined to interfere under Article 226 of the Constitution of India.
judgmentText:
IN THE HIGH COURT OF DELHI AT NEW DELHI
W.P.(C) No. 4821 of 2022

RAJESH KUMAR SHARMA ... Petitioner
versus
INFOTECH SOLUTIONS PVT. LTD. & ANR. ... Respondents

Date of Decision: 14th March, 2023

CORAM: HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ANIL KUMAR MEHTA

JUDGMENT

1. The present writ petition has been filed under Article 226 of the Constitution of India challenging the Award dated 03.08.2022 passed by the Presiding Officer, Labour Court No. III, Delhi, whereby the claim of the petitioner for reinstatement with back wages was rejected.

2. The petitioner was employed as a Senior Software Analyst with the respondent company since 14th February 2016. He was dismissed from service vide order dated 22nd November 2020 following a domestic enquiry conducted by the respondent company on charges of misconduct, specifically unauthorized disclosure of confidential client data to a rival organization.

3. The petitioner contended that the enquiry was vitiated by procedural irregularities, that he was not given adequate opportunity to cross-examine witnesses, and that the punishment of dismissal was disproportionate to the alleged misconduct.

4. The respondent company submitted that the petitioner was issued show cause notices on 01.09.2020, 15.09.2020, and 30.09.2020, and was given full opportunity to respond. A duly constituted Enquiry Officer conducted proceedings over multiple sittings. The petitioner was represented by a co-worker of his choice as per the Standing Orders. Electronic evidence in the form of email exchanges from the petitioner's official account to an employee of the competitor firm was placed on record and remained unrebutted.

5. The Labour Court, after careful appreciation of the evidence on record, held that the charges of misconduct were duly proved, that the enquiry was conducted in a fair and proper manner, and that the punishment was commensurate with the gravity of misconduct. The Court found no infirmity in the said findings.

6. The settled legal position as enunciated by the Hon'ble Supreme Court in Bharat Forge Co. Ltd. v. Uttam Manohar Nakate (2005) 2 SCC 489 and reiterated in various subsequent decisions is that in exercise of powers under Article 226, the High Court does not act as an appellate court over the findings of the Labour Court. Interference is warranted only when there is a jurisdictional error, perversity, or violation of principles of natural justice.

7. This Court has carefully examined the record. The enquiry proceedings demonstrate that the petitioner was afforded reasonable opportunity at every stage. The finding of misconduct is based on documentary evidence which was duly exhibited and considered. The petitioner's denial, in the absence of any cogent rebuttal, was rightly not accepted by the Enquiry Officer and subsequently by the Labour Court.

8. The petitioner's argument regarding proportionality of punishment is also not sustainable. Sharing of confidential client information with a competitor goes to the root of the employment relationship and constitutes a serious breach of trust. In such circumstances, the punishment of dismissal cannot be said to be shockingly disproportionate so as to warrant judicial interference.

9. For the foregoing reasons, this Court finds no merit in the present writ petition. The Award dated 03.08.2022 passed by the Labour Court No. III, Delhi is hereby upheld. The writ petition is dismissed. No order as to costs.

ANIL KUMAR MEHTA, J.
MARCH 14, 2023
